4. MERMAID SAFETY (CRITICAL — many diagrams): scan EVERY ```mermaid block; FAIL any label/edge-text containing breaking chars — parentheses, a colon, a semicolon, slashes, quotes, angle brackets, curly braces. Report the exact offending line. Confirm flowchart TD for multi-path.

1	"""Capacity math for a Real-Time Error Log Monitoring System.
2	All numbers rounded to talkable figures at the end. Only rounded results are surfaced.
3	"""
4	
5	def r(n):
6	    """Round to a talkable magnitude string."""
7	    if n >= 1e12: return f"~{n/1e12:.1f}T"
8	    if n >= 1e9:  return f"~{n/1e9:.1f}B"
9	    if n >= 1e6:  return f"~{n/1e6:.1f}M"
10	    if n >= 1e3:  return f"~{n/1e3:.0f}K"
11	    return f"~{n:.0f}"
12	
13	SEC_PER_DAY = 86_400
14	
15	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
16	# 0. Assumptions (locked in scope lock)
17	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
18	N_SERVICES        = 5_000          # services emitting logs across the fleet
19	HOSTS_PER_SVC     = 20             # avg instances/pods per service
20	N_HOSTS           = N_SERVICES * HOSTS_PER_SVC   # 100K hosts
21	LINES_PER_HOST_S  = 50             # avg log lines per host per second (steady state)
22	AVG_LINE_BYTES    = 600            # avg structured log line incl JSON envelope + fields
23	BURST_FACTOR      = 5              # incident burst: error storms multiply emission
24	RETENTION_DAYS_HOT  = 7            # searchable hot tier
25	RETENTION_DAYS_WARM = 30           # queryable but slower
26	RETENTION_DAYS_COLD = 365          # archived object storage (compliance)
27	
28	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
29	# 1. Ingest throughput (the firehose) — events/sec
30	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
31	avg_eps = N_HOSTS * LINES_PER_HOST_S
32	peak_eps = avg_eps * BURST_FACTOR
33	print("=== INGEST THROUGHPUT (events/sec) ===")
34	print(f"hosts                 = {N_HOSTS:,}")
35	print(f"avg eps               = {N_HOSTS:,} hosts x {LINES_PER_HOST_S}/s = {avg_eps:,}  -> {r(avg_eps)}/s")
36	print(f"peak eps (x{BURST_FACTOR} burst) = {peak_eps:,}  -> {r(peak_eps)}/s")
37	
38	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
39	# 2. Ingest bandwidth (bytes/sec) — sizes Kafka and network
40	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
41	avg_bytes_s = avg_eps * AVG_LINE_BYTES
42	peak_bytes_s = peak_eps * AVG_LINE_BYTES
43	# compression ratio on structured logs is good (repetitive fields)
44	COMPRESS = 0.20  # gzip/zstd ~5:1 on structured logs
45	avg_wire_s = avg_bytes_s * COMPRESS
46	peak_wire_s = peak_bytes_s * COMPRESS
47	print("\n=== INGEST BANDWIDTH ===")
48	print(f"avg raw   = {avg_bytes_s/1e9:.2f} GB/s ({avg_bytes_s*8/1e9:.1f} Gbps)")
49	print(f"peak raw  = {peak_bytes_s/1e9:.2f} GB/s ({peak_bytes_s*8/1e9:.1f} Gbps)")
50	print(f"avg wire (zstd ~5:1) = {avg_wire_s/1e9:.2f} GB/s")
51	print(f"peak wire            = {peak_wire_s/1e9:.2f} GB/s")
52	
53	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
54	# 3. Kafka partition sizing — the binding bottleneck candidate
55	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
56	# A single Kafka partition sustains ~10 MB/s comfortably (one consumer/ordering unit).
57	PARTITION_MBPS = 10
58	peak_wire_mbps = peak_wire_s / 1e6
59	parts_needed = peak_wire_mbps / PARTITION_MBPS
60	print("\n=== KAFKA PARTITION SIZING ===")
61	print(f"peak wire = {peak_wire_mbps:.0f} MB/s ; per-partition cap = {PARTITION_MBPS} MB/s")
62	print(f"partitions needed (peak) = {peak_wire_mbps:.0f}/{PARTITION_MBPS} = {parts_needed:.0f}  -> round up to {r(parts_needed)}")
63	
64	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
65	# 4. Index write rate — what the indexer/Elasticsearch must absorb
66	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
67	# Not everything is indexed at full fidelity; sampling under load. But size to peak EPS.
68	# One ES data node sustains ~10K-20K index docs/s (with our doc size + replication).
69	ES_DOCS_PER_NODE = 15_000
70	es_nodes_peak = peak_eps / ES_DOCS_PER_NODE
71	es_nodes_avg = avg_eps / ES_DOCS_PER_NODE
72	print("\n=== INDEX WRITE (Elasticsearch) ===")
73	print(f"per-node index rate = {ES_DOCS_PER_NODE:,} docs/s")
74	print(f"nodes at avg  = {avg_eps:,}/{ES_DOCS_PER_NODE:,} = {es_nodes_avg:.0f}")
75	print(f"nodes at peak = {peak_eps:,}/{ES_DOCS_PER_NODE:,} = {es_nodes_peak:.0f}  -> {r(es_nodes_peak)} nodes")
76	
77	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
78	# 5. Storage footprint — the huge log store, drives retention $$$
79	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
80	raw_per_day = avg_eps * AVG_LINE_BYTES * SEC_PER_DAY
81	# Index overhead: inverted index + doc values roughly doubles, but compression helps.
82	# Net stored (index, compressed, 1 replica) ~ 0.55x raw; +1 replica => x2 on hot tier.
83	INDEX_FACTOR = 0.55
84	REPLICATION = 2
85	hot_per_day = raw_per_day * INDEX_FACTOR * REPLICATION
86	# Cold archive: compressed raw on object storage, single copy logical (S3 already 11 9s durable)
87	cold_per_day = raw_per_day * COMPRESS
88	
89	hot_total  = hot_per_day  * RETENTION_DAYS_HOT
90	warm_total = hot_per_day  * 0.5 * (RETENTION_DAYS_WARM - RETENTION_DAYS_HOT)  # warm = 1 replica
91	cold_total = cold_per_day * RETENTION_DAYS_COLD
92	
93	print("\n=== STORAGE FOOTPRINT ===")
94	print(f"raw/day                = {raw_per_day/1e12:.1f} TB/day")
95	print(f"hot index/day (idx x{INDEX_FACTOR}, repl x{REPLICATION}) = {hot_per_day/1e12:.2f} TB/day")
96	print(f"HOT  total ({RETENTION_DAYS_HOT}d)  = {hot_total/1e12:.0f} TB  -> {r(hot_total)}B bytes")
97	print(f"WARM total (~{RETENTION_DAYS_WARM-RETENTION_DAYS_HOT}d, 1 repl) = {warm_total/1e12:.0f} TB")
98	print(f"COLD total ({RETENTION_DAYS_COLD}d, S3, zstd) = {cold_total/1e12:.0f} TB = {cold_total/1e15:.2f} PB")
99	
100	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
101	# 6. Storage $ — dominant cost driver
102	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
103	# Hot tier: local NVMe / EBS gp3 ~ $0.10/GB-month effective (provisioned + replicas + headroom)
104	# Cold tier: S3 standard-IA / Glacier ~ $0.004/GB-month
105	HOT_GB_MO  = 0.10
106	WARM_GB_MO = 0.045   # cheaper EBS, fewer replicas
107	COLD_GB_MO = 0.004
108	hot_gb  = hot_total  / 1e9
109	warm_gb = warm_total / 1e9
110	cold_gb = cold_total / 1e9
111	hot_cost  = hot_gb  * HOT_GB_MO
112	warm_cost = warm_gb * WARM_GB_MO
113	cold_cost = cold_gb * COLD_GB_MO
114	store_cost = hot_cost + warm_cost + cold_cost
115	print("\n=== STORAGE COST (monthly) ===")
116	print(f"HOT  {hot_gb/1000:.0f} TB x ${HOT_GB_MO}/GB  = ${hot_cost:,.0f}/mo")
117	print(f"WARM {warm_gb/1000:.0f} TB x ${WARM_GB_MO}/GB = ${warm_cost:,.0f}/mo")
118	print(f"COLD {cold_gb/1000:.0f} TB x ${COLD_GB_MO}/GB = ${cold_cost:,.0f}/mo")
119	print(f"STORAGE TOTAL = ${store_cost:,.0f}/mo  -> {r(store_cost)}/mo")
120	
121	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
122	# 7. Compute $ — indexer + ES + Kafka fleet (order of magnitude)
123	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
124	# ES nodes (size to ~1.5x avg for headroom, not full peak — we sample/shed at peak)
125	es_fleet = int(es_nodes_avg * 1.5)
126	ES_NODE_MO = 1_200    # i3en/r6gd class data node w/ NVMe, fully loaded
127	# Kafka brokers: size to peak bandwidth
128	KAFKA_BROKER_MBPS = 120   # per broker sustainable
129	kafka_brokers = int(peak_wire_mbps / KAFKA_BROKER_MBPS) + 1
130	KAFKA_BROKER_MO = 900
131	# Stateless indexer/ingest workers
132	ingest_workers = int(peak_eps / 50_000) + 1   # ~50K eps per worker
133	WORKER_MO = 250
134	es_cost = es_fleet * ES_NODE_MO
135	kafka_cost = kafka_brokers * KAFKA_BROKER_MO
136	worker_cost = ingest_workers * WORKER_MO
137	compute_cost = es_cost + kafka_cost + worker_cost
138	print("\n=== COMPUTE COST (monthly) ===")
139	print(f"ES data nodes  = {es_fleet} x ${ES_NODE_MO} = ${es_cost:,.0f}/mo")
140	print(f"Kafka brokers  = {kafka_brokers} x ${KAFKA_BROKER_MO} = ${kafka_cost:,.0f}/mo")
141	print(f"Ingest workers = {ingest_workers} x ${WORKER_MO} = ${worker_cost:,.0f}/mo")
142	print(f"COMPUTE TOTAL = ${compute_cost:,.0f}/mo  -> {r(compute_cost)}/mo")
143	
144	total_cost = store_cost + compute_cost
145	print(f"\n=== GRAND TOTAL ~ ${total_cost:,.0f}/mo  -> {r(total_cost)}/mo ===")
146	
147	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
148	# 8. Unit economics — $ per 1M log events ingested
149	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
150	events_per_mo = avg_eps * SEC_PER_DAY * 30
151	cost_per_1m = total_cost / (events_per_mo / 1e6)
152	print("\n=== UNIT ECONOMICS ===")
153	print(f"events/month = {events_per_mo/1e9:.0f}B")
154	print(f"cost per 1M events ingested = ${cost_per_1m:.4f}")
155	print(f"  -> talkable: ~${cost_per_1m*100:.1f} cents per 1M events, or ~${total_cost/(events_per_mo/1e9):.0f} per 1B events")
156	
157	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
158	# 9. Search query QPS — the read path (sized from MISS, i.e. uncached queries)
159	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
160	N_ENGINEERS = 3_000           # engineers + dashboards hitting search
161	QUERIES_PER_ENG_DAY = 40      # interactive searches + dashboard refreshes
162	DASHBOARD_PANELS = 2_000      # live dashboard panels auto-refreshing
163	PANEL_REFRESH_S = 30          # each panel refreshes every 30s
164	interactive_qps = N_ENGINEERS * QUERIES_PER_ENG_DAY / SEC_PER_DAY
165	dashboard_qps = DASHBOARD_PANELS / PANEL_REFRESH_S
166	CACHE_HIT = 0.70              # dashboard queries are highly repetitive -> cacheable
167	total_search_qps = interactive_qps + dashboard_qps
168	miss_qps = total_search_qps * (1 - CACHE_HIT)
169	print("\n=== SEARCH QUERY QPS (read path) ===")
170	print(f"interactive = {N_ENGINEERS}x{QUERIES_PER_ENG_DAY}/86400 = {interactive_qps:.1f}/s")
171	print(f"dashboards  = {DASHBOARD_PANELS}/{PANEL_REFRESH_S}s = {dashboard_qps:.0f}/s")
172	print(f"total search QPS = {total_search_qps:.0f}/s  -> {r(total_search_qps)}/s")
173	print(f"MISS QPS (cache hit {CACHE_HIT:.0%}) = {miss_qps:.0f}/s  -> {r(miss_qps)}/s  (this is what ES query layer sees)")
174	print(f"cold-cache worst case (0% hit) = {total_search_qps:.0f}/s")
175	
176	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
177	# 10. Read:write ratio — drives where we spend
178	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
179	ratio = avg_eps / total_search_qps
180	print("\n=== READ:WRITE RATIO ===")
181	print(f"writes (ingest) {avg_eps:,}/s : reads (search) {total_search_qps:.0f}/s")
182	print(f"ratio ~ {ratio:.0f}:1 WRITE-heavy (inverse of typical web)  -> spend budget on INGEST + STORAGE")
183	
184	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
185	# 11. Alerting fan-in — aggregation cardinality
186	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
187	ALERT_RULES = 10_000          # rules across all teams
188	EVAL_INTERVAL_S = 60          # evaluate each rule every minute
189	alert_evals_s = ALERT_RULES / EVAL_INTERVAL_S
190	print("\n=== ALERTING ===")
191	print(f"alert rules = {ALERT_RULES:,} ; eval every {EVAL_INTERVAL_S}s")
192	print(f"rule evaluations/s = {alert_evals_s:.0f}/s (against pre-aggregated counters, NOT raw logs)")
193

1	# Design a Real-Time Error Log Monitoring System
2	
3	*Ingest logs and errors from thousands of services, index them for search, alert on error spikes, power dashboards. The defining tension: this is one of the few systems where **writes dwarf reads by ~70,000 to 1** — so almost everything we'd do for a normal web service is inverted.*
4	
5	---
6	
7	## 1. Scope, Requirements & Assumptions
8	
9	> 🎙️ "Here's how I'll structure this: I'll lock scope with a couple of quick assumptions, state the requirements and rough scale, sketch the API and data model, draw the high-level design one requirement at a time, then go deep on the two or three hardest parts — ingestion, the search index, and retention cost — checking in with you as I go. Sound good?"
10	
11	The core tension in one line: a log monitoring system is a **firehose, not a web app**. Reads are rare and writes are relentless, so the whole design budget goes to ingestion durability, index throughput, and storage cost — and the hardest senior question isn't latency, it's *"what do you throw away, and when?"* Since the recruiter flagged an Amazon-style bar, I'll keep **cost and operations first-class** the entire way.
12	
13	### 🔒 Scope lock (assumptions first — veto any of these)
14	
15	I'll state the four decisions that fork the architecture as assumptions. Stop me if any is wrong.
16	
17	1. **Scale: ~5,000 services on ~100,000 hosts emitting ~5M log events/second average, bursting ~5x to ~25M/s during incidents.** This is the single number that drives everything. If you mean a few thousand events/s, this is a much simpler box.
18	2. **This is an observability/operations tool, not a compliance audit log — so under overload, I'm allowed to SAMPLE and DROP low-value logs to protect the system.** This is the most consequential assumption: it means the ingest path is best-effort, not exactly-once. If logs are legally required (SOX, PCI audit trails), the design changes fundamentally.
19	3. **Search is "good enough" near-real-time: a log is searchable within a few seconds of being emitted, not instantly.** This buys us asynchronous, buffered indexing instead of synchronous writes.
20	4. **Multi-region for ingestion locality and resilience, but a single logical search/alert plane.** Agents ship to the nearest region; queries can span regions. I'll start single-region in the diagrams and note the multi-region delta.
21	
22	> 🤝 **In scope:** ingest pipeline, full-text + structured search, error-rate alerting, dashboards, tiered retention. **Out of scope:** distributed tracing spans (different shape), metrics time-series (Prometheus-style — I'll note where they overlap), the agent's on-host collection internals, and billing. Add or cut before I commit?
23	
24	### Functional requirements (above the line — derived from the assumptions)
25	
26	Since we assumed a write-heavy firehose that's allowed to sample, the core flows are:
27	
28	**FR1 — Ingest logs from thousands of services.** Agents on every host ship structured log events to the platform.
29	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** every server in the company has a little program that scoops up its log lines and sends them to us, and we have to swallow all of it without falling over.
30	- ⚡ **Why it's hard:** ~5M events/s steady, ~25M/s in a storm — and the storm is exactly when something is on fire and engineers need the logs most. The pipe must not collapse under the burst it exists to capture.
31	
32	**FR2 — Search and filter logs.** Engineers query logs by free text, service, level, time range, and structured fields.
33	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** an engineer types "payment-service errors in the last 10 minutes containing timeout" and gets results in under a second.
34	- ⚡ **Why it's hard:** full-text search over trillions of rows means an inverted index, and that index has to be *built* at 5M docs/s while staying queryable.
35	
36	**FR3 — Alert on error spikes.** Teams define rules ("error rate for checkout > 5% over 2 minutes") and get paged when breached.
37	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** if errors suddenly jump for a service, we wake someone up before customers complain.
38	- ⚡ **Why it's hard:** you cannot scan raw logs every 60 seconds to count errors — you must pre-aggregate counts as data streams in, or alerting becomes its own DDoS on the index.
39	
40	**FR4 — Dashboards.** Live charts of error rates, volumes, and top errors per service.
41	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** a wall of graphs showing how healthy each service is right now.
42	- ⚡ **Why it's hard:** thousands of panels auto-refreshing turn into a repetitive query storm; the answer is pre-aggregation + caching, not hammering the index.
43	
44	**FR5 — Tiered retention and lifecycle.** Keep recent logs hot and searchable, age older logs to cheap storage, expire per policy.
45	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** last week's logs are instantly searchable; last year's sit in cheap cold storage for compliance; older than that is gone.
46	- ⚡ **Why it's hard:** at ~260 TB/day raw, naive "keep everything hot forever" is a multi-million-dollar-a-month mistake. Retention IS the cost-control lever.
47	
48	**Below the line (out of scope):** trace/span correlation, anomaly-detection ML, log parsing/grok rule management UI, RBAC editor internals, agent auto-update. I'll mention each only if it touches the hot path.
49	
50	### Non-functional requirements (provisional targets, tagged to assumptions)
51	
52	**NFR1 — Ingest durability once buffered: lose ~0 events after they hit the durable buffer; best-effort before that.** (gated on assumption 2)
53	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** once a log lands in our queue it won't be lost, but on the host before that, under extreme overload, we may drop some on purpose.
54	- 💥 **What breaks without it:** if the buffer can lose data, the post-incident investigation has holes exactly where you need detail.
55	
56	**NFR2 — Ingest-to-searchable lag p99 ~ 5 seconds.** (gated on assumption 3)
57	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** at most a 5-second delay between a server logging an error and you being able to search it.
58	- 💥 **What breaks without it:** if it's minutes, engineers tail the raw host logs instead and the tool is useless during an incident.
59	
60	**NFR3 — Search query latency p99 ~ 1 second** for a 15-minute window; degrades gracefully for wider windows.
61	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** a normal "what's broken now" search feels instant.
62	- 💥 **What breaks without it:** slow search during an outage means a slower outage.
63	
64	**NFR4 — Alert detection latency ~ 60-90 seconds** from spike onset to page.
65	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** we notice a spike within a minute or two, not after the customer tweets.
66	- 💥 **What breaks without it:** the whole point of the system — early warning — is gone.
67	
68	**NFR5 — Availability: ingest 99.9% ("three nines"), query 99.5%.** Ingest matters more than query.
69	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** the firehose almost never stops swallowing; the search UI can blink for a few minutes a month.
70	- 💥 **What breaks without it:** if ingest drops, you're blind during the exact window you most need sight.
71

Five thousand services, a hundred thousand hosts, five million log events a second normally and five times that in an incident. Five things to build: ingest the firehose, search it, alert on error spikes, drive dashboards, and age the data out cheaply. The non-functionals I care about most are ingest durability once buffered and a five-second searchable lag — and crucially, I'm assuming this is an ops tool that's allowed to sample under load, not a legal audit log. 80	## 2. The Forks Behind My Assumptions (what changes if I'm wrong)
81	
82	These are the residual forks from §1 — the three places my design is most sensitive. I'm not re-asking scale; I locked that at 5M/s.
83	
84	**Fork A — I assumed sampling/dropping is allowed (ops tool, not audit log).**
85	- ↔️ **What changes if I'm wrong:**
86	  - *If audit/compliance* → I need a second, **exactly-once immutable path**: every event acknowledged durably before the agent moves on, WORM object storage, crypto-shredding for right-to-erasure, no load-shedding ever. That's a different, more expensive system bolted alongside.
87	  - *If ops tool (my assumption)* → ingest is best-effort, I can shed low-severity logs under overload, and I keep error/fatal logs preferentially. Far cheaper.
88	- 🗣️ **How I'd say it:** "I'm treating this as an operations tool that can drop debug logs when it's drowning. If any of these logs are legally required, stop me — that's a separate guaranteed pipeline and roughly doubles cost."
89	
90	**Fork B — I assumed near-real-time (5s lag) is fine, not instant.**
91	- ↔️ **What changes if I'm wrong:**
92	  - *If instant (<100ms)* → I'd have to index synchronously on the write path, which can't keep up at 5M/s — I'd push toward in-memory ring buffers per host and accept a far smaller searchable window.
93	  - *If 5s is fine (my assumption)* → I buffer in Kafka and index asynchronously, smoothing bursts and decoupling producers from the index.
94	- 🗣️ **How I'd say it:** "I'm buying a few seconds of indexing lag to decouple the firehose from the index. If you need truly instant search I'll trade away some scale for it."
95	
96	**Fork C — I assumed structured logs (JSON with known fields), not arbitrary free text.**
97	- ↔️ **What changes if I'm wrong:**
98	  - *If arbitrary text* → heavier full-text tokenization, larger inverted index, weaker filtering — pushes index cost up and field-based alerting gets fuzzy.
99	  - *If structured (my assumption)* → I index a few hot fields (service, level, trace_id, message) and store the rest as non-indexed payload, keeping the index lean.
100	- 🗣️ **How I'd say it:** "I'm assuming logs are mostly structured JSON so I only index the fields people actually filter on. If they're freeform, the index gets bigger and I'd sample harder."
101	
102	> 🤝 **Checkpoint:** "Those are my three pressure points — sampling allowed, a few seconds of lag, structured logs. Happy with all three, or should I re-tilt one before I size it?"

106	## 3. Scale & Capacity (talkable numbers)
107	
108	I ran the arithmetic in a small script; here are only the numbers that drive a decision. (**QPS** = queries per second; **EPS** = events per second; **TTL** = time-to-live, how long a cached value lives before it expires.)
109	
110	| Metric | Derivation (step-by-step) | Rounded | Talkable phrase | Decision it drives |
111	|---|---|---|---|---|
112	| **Avg ingest** | 100K hosts x 50 lines/s = 5,000,000/s | ~5M EPS | "about five million events a second" | Write-path is THE system |
113	| **Peak ingest** | 5M x 5 burst factor = 25,000,000/s | ~25M EPS | "twenty-five million in a storm" | Must buffer + shed, not scale synchronously |
114	| **Ingest bandwidth (peak, compressed)** | 25M x 600 bytes x 0.2 zstd = 3 GB/s | ~3 GB/s | "three gigabytes a second over the wire" | Kafka + network sizing |
115	| **Kafka partitions** | 3000 MB/s ÷ 10 MB/s per partition = 300 | ~300 | "a few hundred partitions" | Parallelism + ordering unit count |
116	| **ES index nodes (peak)** | 25M ÷ 15K docs/s per node = 1,667 | ~1,700 | "roughly seventeen hundred index nodes at full peak" | **The binding bottleneck** → sample at peak |
117	| **Raw volume** | 5M x 600 bytes x 86,400s = 259 TB/day | ~260 TB/day | "a quarter petabyte a day" | Retention is the cost lever |
118	| **Hot store (7d, indexed, 2 replicas)** | 285 TB/day x 7 = ~2,000 TB | ~2 PB | "two petabytes hot" | Tiering required |
119	| **Cold archive (365d, compressed)** | 52 TB/day x 365 = ~19 PB | ~19 PB | "nearly twenty petabytes cold" | Glacier-class storage only |
120	| **Storage cost** | hot+warm+cold across tiers | ~$420K/mo | "north of four hundred grand a month just to store" | Dominant ops cost |
121	| **Compute cost** | ES + Kafka + workers | ~$750K/mo | "three-quarters of a million in compute" | Sampling pays for itself |
122	| **Total** | storage + compute | **~$1.2M/mo** | "about a million two a month" | Cost is a first-class constraint |
123	| **Unit economics** | $1.2M ÷ 13 trillion events/mo | **~$0.09 / 1M events** | "nine cents per million logs, ~$90 per billion" | The number to optimize |
124	| **Search QPS** | 1.4 interactive + 67 dashboard ≈ 68/s | ~70/s | "about seventy searches a second" | Read tier is tiny |
125	| **Search MISS QPS** | 68 x (1 − 0.70 cache hit) = 20/s | ~20/s | "twenty uncached queries a second" | ES query layer trivially small |
126	| **Read:write ratio** | 5,000,000 ÷ 68 | **~73,000:1** | "seventy thousand to one, write-heavy" | Invert the usual playbook |
127	| **Alert evals** | 10K rules ÷ 60s | ~170/s | "a couple hundred rule checks a second" | Must hit pre-aggregates, not raw logs |
128	
129	**The ONE decision-forcing number:** ~25M peak EPS would need ~1,700 ES index nodes to absorb at full fidelity. That's the binding bottleneck. **Flip threshold:** if sustained ingest exceeds what ~500 nodes (our headroom-sized fleet, ~7.5M docs/s) can take, we **sample** — drop debug/info first, keep error/fatal. So we provision for ~1.5x average, not for peak, and shed the difference. That single choice saves us from a 3x bigger ES fleet.
130	
131	Storage is NOT a non-issue here (it usually is) — at ~$420K/mo it's the second-biggest line and the reason retention tiering exists.
132	
133	> 🎙️ **Script:** "The number that runs this design is five million events a second, twenty-five at peak. And here's the kicker — search is only about seventy queries a second, so the read-to-write ratio is something like seventy thousand to one. That's the opposite of a normal web service. So I'm spending my entire budget on the write path and on storage — the read tier is almost free — and at peak I sample rather than buy seventeen hundred index nodes."

137	## 4. Core Entities
138	
139	This is a first-draft scaffold — full fields land in §7.
140	
141	**LogEvent** — the atomic unit: one structured log line. Owns its timestamp, service, level, message, and arbitrary structured fields.
142	- 🗣️ **Analogy:** think of it like a single line in a giant shared diary — who wrote it, when, how bad it was, and what they said.
143	- ⚠️ **Probe:** "Is this ever updated?" → No. LogEvents are immutable, append-only, write-once. That's what lets us use append-optimized stores and partition-drop deletes.
144	
145	**Service** — a logical application emitting logs (e.g. `checkout-api`). Owns its identity, team owner, and log-volume profile.
146	- 🗣️ **Analogy:** like a department in the company — each one files its own logs and has an owner you page.
147	- ⚠️ **Probe:** "How do you stop one noisy service from drowning the others?" → per-service quotas and per-service Kafka partitioning so one bad deploy can't starve everyone.
148	
149	**AlertRule** — a team-defined condition over aggregated error rates (threshold, window, target service).
150	- 🗣️ **Analogy:** like a smoke detector you configure — "beep if smoke in the kitchen exceeds this level for two minutes."
151	- ⚠️ **Probe:** "How do you evaluate 10,000 rules cheaply?" → against pre-aggregated per-service-per-minute counters, never against raw logs.
152	
153	**Aggregate (rollup)** — a pre-computed counter: error count per service per level per minute. Owns the materialized counts that power both alerts and dashboards.
154	- 🗣️ **Analogy:** like a running tally sheet at a turnstile — you don't recount everyone who came through, you just read the counter.
155	- ⚠️ **Probe:** "Why not compute on read?" → because alerting on read would re-scan trillions of rows every minute; we compute counts once as data streams in.
156	
157	**RetentionPolicy** — per-service or per-tier rules: how long logs stay hot, warm, cold, and when they expire.
158	- 🗣️ **Analogy:** like a filing cabinet schedule — this drawer for a week, the basement for a year, then the shredder.
159	- ⚠️ **Probe:** "How do you delete a petabyte cheaply?" → drop whole time-partitions, never row-by-row DELETE.
160	
161	> 🎙️ **Script:** "Five entities. The one that matters most and surprises people is the Aggregate — the pre-computed counter. Alerting and dashboards never touch raw logs; they read these rollups, which is the only way ten thousand alert rules stay cheap. The LogEvent itself is dead simple and, importantly, immutable. I'll flesh out the fields, the shard keys, and which store each lives in when I get to the data model."

164	
165	## 5. API / Interface
166	
167	All endpoints are versioned under **`/v1`** (additive fields don't bump the version; breaking changes go to `/v2`). List endpoints use **cursor pagination** — an opaque `next_cursor` keyset token, not offset/limit, because offset gets slower as it scans and can skip or duplicate rows as new logs stream in under the query. The caller's identity comes from a **JWT** *(JSON Web Token — a signed token that proves who you are without a DB lookup)*; we never trust a client-supplied service id or timestamp for authz.
168	
169	### Ingest — FR1
170	**`POST /v1/ingest`** — POST because it creates new state (appends events). This is the firehose endpoint, called by agents, batched.
171	- `events: LogEvent[]` — a batch of log lines (agents batch ~1000 to amortize overhead).
172	- `agent_id: string` — identifies the shipping agent; used for quota + auth, NOT trusted as the log source of truth.
173	- `batch_id: string` — **idempotency key** so a retried batch isn't double-indexed.
174	- The server sets `received_at` and the partition assignment; the client cannot forge ingestion time.
175	
176	```
177	// Request
178	POST /v1/ingest
179	{
180	  "agent_id": "agt_host_8821",
181	  "batch_id": "b_2026-06-21T10-00-03_8821_004",
182	  "events": [
183	    { "ts": "2026-06-21T10:00:02.991Z", "service": "checkout-api", "level": "ERROR",
184	      "message": "payment timeout", "trace_id": "tr_9af2", "fields": { "user_tier": "gold" } }
185	  ]
186	}
187	
188	// Response 202
189	{ "accepted": 1, "dropped": 0, "batch_id": "b_2026-06-21T10-00-03_8821_004" }
190	```
191	- **Key decision:** returns **202 Accepted**, not 200 — the events are durably buffered, not yet indexed. Under overload it returns `{"accepted": 800, "dropped": 200}` so the agent learns it's being sampled and can back off. Idempotency on `batch_id` (dedup store, 1-hour TTL) means a network retry never double-counts.
192	
193	### Search — FR2
194	**`GET /v1/search`** — GET because it only reads.
195	- `q: string` — the query (free text + field filters, e.g. `level:ERROR service:checkout-api timeout`).
196	- `from, to: timestamp` — time window; narrow windows hit fewer index shards.
197	- `cursor: string` — keyset pagination token.
198	
199	```
200	GET /v1/search?q=level:ERROR%20service:checkout-api%20timeout&from=2026-06-21T09:50:00Z&to=2026-06-21T10:00:00Z&limit=50
201	
202	// Response 200
203	{
204	  "hits": [ { "ts": "2026-06-21T09:58:11Z", "service": "checkout-api", "level": "ERROR",
205	              "message": "payment timeout", "trace_id": "tr_9af2" } ],
206	  "total_estimate": 412,
207	  "next_cursor": "eyJ0cyI6IjIwMjYtMDYtMjFUMDk6NTg6MTFaIn0"
208	}
209	```
210	- **Key decision:** `total_estimate`, not exact total — counting all matches across trillions of rows is prohibitively expensive, so we return an approximate count. **Object-level authz:** the JWT's team claims must include `checkout-api`'s owning team (or a global-read role), enforced server-side — an engineer can't read another team's logs by editing the query.
211	
212	### Alerts — FR3
213	**`POST /v1/alert-rules`** — POST creates a new rule. **`GET /v1/alert-rules`** lists them (cursor-paginated).
214	- `target: {service, level}` — what to watch.
215	- `condition: {metric, op, threshold, window_s}` — e.g. error_rate > 0.05 over 120s.
216	- `notify: [channel]` — PagerDuty/Slack targets.
217	- Server sets `rule_id`, `created_by` (from JWT), `state` — the client can't forge ownership.
218	
219	```
220	// Request
221	POST /v1/alert-rules
222	{ "target": {"service": "checkout-api", "level": "ERROR"},
223	  "condition": {"metric": "error_rate", "op": ">", "threshold": 0.05, "window_s": 120},
224	  "notify": ["pagerduty:checkout-oncall"] }
225	
226	// Response 201
227	{ "rule_id": "rule_77a1", "state": "ACTIVE", "created_by": "u_anshul" }
228	```
229	- **Key decision:** rules evaluate against **pre-aggregated counters**, declared at create-time so we know which rollups to materialize. **Object-level authz:** you can only create rules for services your team owns.
230	
231	### Dashboards — FR4
232	**`GET /v1/aggregates`** — GET, reads rollups for charts.
233	- `service, metric, window, step` — which rollup series and bucket size.
234	
235	```
236	GET /v1/aggregates?service=checkout-api&metric=error_rate&from=...&to=...&step=60s
237	// Response 200
238	{ "series": [ {"t": "2026-06-21T09:58:00Z", "value": 0.061}, {"t": "2026-06-21T09:59:00Z", "value": 0.058} ] }
239	```
240	- **Key decision:** dashboards read **only** rollups, never `/v1/search`, so 2,000 auto-refreshing panels can't storm the index. Cached with a 30s TTL matching the refresh interval.
241	
242	> 💬 "Notice ingest returns 202 with a dropped count — that's me being honest with the agent that I sampled it, instead of silently lying with a 200."
243	
244	> 🎙️ **Script:** "Four endpoints. Ingest is the firehose — agents POST batches, I return 202 with an accepted-and-dropped count so back-pressure is visible. Search is a plain GET against the index with cursor pagination and an estimated total, because exact counts over trillions of rows are too expensive. Alert rules and dashboards both read pre-aggregated counters, never raw logs — that's the trick that keeps them cheap. Everywhere, identity comes from the JWT and I check the caller actually owns the service they're touching."
245

248	## 6. High-Level Design (one requirement at a time)
249	
250	Color convention used in every diagram: blue = clients/agents, green = our services, amber = queues/streams, grey = datastores, purple = external.
251	
252	### 6.1 "Ingest logs from thousands of services"
253	
254	The firehose. The whole art is decoupling the producers (100K hosts) from the index so a burst doesn't crash anything downstream.
255	
256	**ARCHITECTURE DECISIONS TABLE**
257	
258	| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off we accept |
259	|---|---|---|---|---|
260	| Host agent | A small daemon on every host that tails log files and ships batches | Needs a local buffer + back-pressure so it doesn't OOM the host during a stall | Not direct app→index writes (couples every app to ES; one slow index stalls all apps) | Agent can drop on-host under extreme local backlog |
261	| Ingest gateway | Stateless service that authenticates batches and writes to Kafka | Needs a thin, horizontally scalable front door that does auth + quota + sampling | Not writing to ES directly (no buffer; index becomes the bottleneck at 5M/s) | One extra hop of latency |
262	| Kafka | A durable, ordered log that buffers a firehose of events so nothing is lost if a consumer is slow | Absorbs the 5x burst, decouples producers from indexers, replays on failure | Not SQS/RabbitMQ (lower sustained throughput, weaker replay); not in-memory queue (loses data on crash) | Operational complexity of running brokers |
263	| Indexer workers | Stateless consumers that read Kafka, transform, and bulk-write to ES | Lets us scale index throughput independently and apply sampling at one place | Not Logstash-as-monolith (heavier, harder to scale the hot path) | Another fleet to operate |
264	
265	> 🎙️ **Narration:** "Every host runs an agent that tails logs and ships batches — I deliberately don't let apps write to the index directly, because then one slow index stalls every app. The agent hits a stateless ingest gateway that does auth, per-service quota, and sampling, then writes into Kafka — a durable ordered log that soaks up the burst. Indexer workers read off Kafka at their own pace and bulk-write into the search index. The trade-off is more moving parts, but the payoff is that the firehose and the index are completely decoupled."
266	
267	> **🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
268	> **Host agent** — a lightweight program on each server that collects log lines and forwards them. Here it gives us an on-host buffer so a network blip doesn't lose logs.
269	> **Kafka** — a durable append-only log that many producers write to and many consumers read from at their own speed. Here it buffers the 25M/s burst so the index never sees the spike directly.
270	> **Back-pressure** — the mechanism by which a slow downstream tells an upstream to slow down. Here the gateway's 202-with-dropped-count tells agents they're being sampled.
271	> **Bulk write** — sending many documents to the index in one request instead of one-at-a-time. Here it's the only way to hit millions of docs/s.
272	
273	**DIAGRAM — Ingest firehose**
274	```mermaid
275	flowchart TD
276	    A[Host agents x100K]
277	    A -->|"1 POST batch"| GW[Ingest gateway]
278	    GW -->|"2 auth and quota"| GW
279	    GW -->|"3 produce events"| K[[Kafka log buffer]]
280	    K -->|"4 consume at own pace"| IX[Indexer workers]
281	    IX -->|"5 bulk index"| ES[(Search index)]
282	    IX -->|"6 emit raw"| OBJ[(Cold object store)]
283	    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,color:#e6edf3
284	    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,color:#e6edf3
285	    classDef queue fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,color:#e6edf3
286	    classDef db fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,color:#e6edf3
287	    class A client
288	    class GW,IX svc
289	    class K queue
290	    class ES,OBJ db
291	```
292	↳ new components: agent, gateway, Kafka, indexer, search index, cold store.
293	
294	**NUMBERED STEP NARRATION**
295	1. **Agent POSTs a batch** of ~1000 events to the gateway. *Why the agent:* it owns the on-host buffer and back-pressure. *State:* events leave the host. *What goes wrong:* network stall → agent buffers locally, then drops debug-level first if the buffer fills.
296	2. **Gateway authenticates and applies quota/sampling.** *Why here:* one central place to enforce per-service limits and shed load. *State:* batch is admitted (maybe partially). *What goes wrong:* overload → gateway samples and returns a dropped count.
297	3. **Gateway produces to Kafka,** partitioned by service. *Why Kafka:* durable buffer that absorbs bursts. *State:* events are now durably persisted (replicated x3). *What goes wrong:* a broker dies → another replica serves; producer retries are idempotent on batch_id.
298	4. **Indexer workers consume** at their own throughput. *Why decoupled:* the index can lag without back-pressuring producers. *State:* events in flight to the index. *What goes wrong:* indexer lag grows → we add workers or sample harder; Kafka holds the backlog.
299	5. **Workers bulk-index into the search index.** *Why bulk:* only way to hit millions of docs/s. *State:* events become searchable (~5s lag). *What goes wrong:* index rejects writes (full) → workers pause, Kafka buffers, alarms fire.
300	6. **Workers also tee raw events to cold object storage** (compressed). *Why:* cheap durable archive independent of the index for replay and compliance. *State:* permanent copy exists. *What goes wrong:* object store slow → buffered async, never blocks the index.
301	
302	> 💬 "The one insight: I never let an app talk to the index. Kafka is the shock absorber between a firehose that bursts 5x and an index that has a fixed write ceiling."
303	
304	> 🎙️ **Script:** "Agents ship batches to a stateless gateway that auths and samples, then drops everything into Kafka — a durable log that buffers the burst. Indexer workers pull from Kafka at whatever rate they can sustain and bulk-write into the search index, and tee a compressed copy to cold object storage. The decision that makes this work is decoupling: Kafka means a five-times burst inflates the queue, not the index, and the index never has to scale to peak."
305	
306	### 6.2 "Search and filter logs"
307	
308	Now the read path — tiny in QPS, but it must search an inverted index that's being written at 5M docs/s.
309	
310	**ARCHITECTURE DECISIONS TABLE**
311	
312	| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off we accept |
313	|---|---|---|---|---|
314	| Search index (ES/OpenSearch) | A search engine that pre-builds an inverted index — a map of every word to the events containing it | Need ranked full-text + field filters over trillions of rows in <1s; only an inverted index can | Not SQL LIKE (scans every row — unusable at this scale); not grep over files (no index, minutes per query) | Index lags raw by ~5s; expensive to store |
315	| Query service | Stateless API that fans a query across time-relevant index shards and merges results | Routes to only the shards covering the time window, enforces authz | Not querying every shard always (wasteful — most queries are last 15 min) | Slightly more routing logic |
316	| Query cache | A short-TTL cache of recent query results | Dashboard + repeated queries are highly cacheable | Not caching nothing (dashboards would storm the index) | Stale up to the TTL (fine for logs) |
317	
318	> 🎙️ **Narration:** "Search runs on an inverted index — think of the index at the back of a book mapping each word to the pages it appears on, except here words map to log events. I picked a search engine like Elasticsearch because no relational database can do ranked full-text over trillions of rows in under a second. A stateless query service fans the query only across the shards covering the requested time window — most queries are 'last fifteen minutes', so it touches a tiny slice. A short-TTL cache absorbs repeated and dashboard queries."
319	
320	> **🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
321	> **Inverted index** — a precomputed map from each term to the list of documents containing it, so search is a lookup, not a scan. Here it turns full-text search over trillions of logs into milliseconds.
322	> **Shard** — one horizontal slice of the index living on one node. Here we shard by time so old data can be moved to cheaper nodes or dropped wholesale.
323	> **Query fan-out** — sending one query to many shards and merging the answers. Here it's scoped to only the shards in the time window.
324	
325	**DIAGRAM — Search read path**
326	```mermaid
327	flowchart TD
328	    U[Engineer browser]
329	    U -->|"1 GET search"| QS[Query service]
330	    QS -->|"2 check cache"| QC[(Query cache)]
331	    QC -.->|"3 miss"| QS
332	    QS -->|"4 fan to time shards"| ES[(Search index)]
333	    ES -->|"5 ranked hits"| QS
334	    QS -->|"6 cache and return"| U
335	    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,color:#e6edf3
336	    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,color:#e6edf3
337	    classDef db fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,color:#e6edf3
338	    class U client
339	    class QS svc
340	    class QC,ES db
341	```
342	↳ reuses existing: search index (from §6.1).
343	
344	**NUMBERED STEP NARRATION**
345	1. **Engineer issues a search.** *Why query service:* it owns authz + routing. *State:* request enters. *What goes wrong:* unauthorized service → 403 before any index hit.
346	2. **Query service checks the cache.** *Why:* dashboards and repeats are common. *State:* cache lookup. *What goes wrong:* cache down → bypass to index, slower but correct.
347	3. **On miss, proceed to the index.** *State:* will hit ES.
348	4. **Fan out only to shards covering the time window.** *Why:* avoids touching cold/old shards. *State:* parallel shard queries. *What goes wrong:* one slow shard → partial results + a "results may be incomplete" flag rather than hanging.
349	5. **Index returns ranked hits.** *State:* merged result set.
350	6. **Cache and return with a cursor.** *State:* cached for 30s; client can page. *What goes wrong:* huge result → we cap and paginate via cursor.
351	
352	> 💬 "Search is only ~20 uncached queries a second — laughably small — so the read tier is almost free. The cost is all in building the index, not querying it."
353	
354	> 🎙️ **Script:** "An engineer searches, the query service checks a short cache, and on a miss fans out only to the index shards covering the time window — because almost every query is 'last fifteen minutes'. Results come back ranked and get cached for thirty seconds. The key point is that reads are trivial here — twenty uncached queries a second — so I spend nothing scaling the read path; the expense is keeping the inverted index built and fresh."
355	
356	### 6.3 "Alert on error spikes"
357	
358	The trap here is alerting by scanning raw logs. We pre-aggregate counts as data streams, then evaluate cheap rules against the counts.
359	
360	**ARCHITECTURE DECISIONS TABLE**
361	
362	| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off we accept |
363	|---|---|---|---|---|
364	| Stream aggregator (Flink) | A stream processor that reads Kafka and maintains running per-service-per-minute error counts | Need continuous aggregation at 5M/s — must compute on the stream, not on read | Not periodic ES count queries (10K rules x scans = a self-DDoS); not cron over raw logs | Approximate counts during late-arriving data |
365	| Aggregate store | A small fast store holding the rollup counters and time-series | Tiny vs raw logs; powers both alerts and dashboards | Not raw-log scans (orders of magnitude more expensive) | Rollups are lossy by design (counts, not lines) |
366	| Alert evaluator | Service that checks the ~10K rules against the rollups each interval | Cheap because it reads counters, not logs | Not evaluating inside each query (couples alerting to UI) | Slight detection delay (~60-90s) |
367	| Notifier | Sends pages/Slack via external systems | Decouples detection from delivery + dedup | Not paging directly from the evaluator (retries/dedup get messy) | Extra hop |
368	
369	> 🎙️ **Narration:** "Alerting is where people get it wrong — they try to count errors by querying the index every minute, and with ten thousand rules that's a self-inflicted denial of service. Instead I use a stream processor, Flink — software that reads the Kafka stream and keeps a live running count of errors per service per minute. Those tiny counters land in an aggregate store. The alert evaluator then just compares ten thousand rules against those counters, which is dirt cheap, and hands breaches to a notifier that dedupes and pages."
370	
371	> **🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
372	> **Flink** — a stream-processing engine that computes running aggregates over an event stream in real time. Here it counts errors per service per minute as logs flow by, so alerting never scans raw data.
373	> **Windowed aggregation** — grouping events into time buckets (e.g. per-minute) and computing a value per bucket. Here it's the per-service error count that rules check.
374	> **Rollup** — a small precomputed summary that replaces scanning the raw data. Here it shrinks trillions of logs to a few counters per service.
375

397	**NUMBERED STEP NARRATION**
398	1. **Aggregator consumes the same Kafka stream** the indexers use. *Why same stream:* one source of truth, no extra read load. *State:* events flow through. *What goes wrong:* aggregator lag → counts trail real-time; bounded by Kafka retention.
399	2. **It writes per-minute rollups** to the aggregate store. *State:* counters updated. *What goes wrong:* late-arriving events → handled with a small grace window; very late events update next bucket.
400	3. **Evaluator reads counters** every interval. *Why:* cheap reads. *State:* rule checks run. *What goes wrong:* evaluator down → standby instance takes over; rules re-evaluate from stored counters.
401	4. **A breach is detected.** *State:* alert state transitions to FIRING. *What goes wrong:* flapping → require N consecutive breaches before paging.

404	> 💬 "The whole alerting cost trick: I count errors once as they stream by, so ten thousand rules read counters, not logs."
405	
406	> 🎙️ **Script:** "Alerting reads the same Kafka stream as indexing, but instead of storing logs it keeps running per-minute error counts in a tiny aggregate store. The evaluator compares ten thousand rules against those counters every interval — cheap, because it's reading numbers, not scanning logs — and on a sustained breach it hands off to a notifier that dedupes and pages. The decision is pre-aggregation: I compute the counts once at write time rather than ten thousand times at read time."
407	
408	### 6.4 "Dashboards"
409	
410	Dashboards reuse the alerting rollups — no new datastore, just read the aggregate store.
411	
412	↳ builds on §6.3 diagram — no new components. The dashboard service reads the **aggregate store** with a 30s cache, exactly the rollups the alert evaluator uses. The only new edge: 2,000 panels → dashboard service → aggregate store (cached). This is why dashboards can't storm the index: they never touch it.
413	
414	> 💬 "Dashboards and alerts share the same counters — build the rollup once, use it twice."
415	
416	### 6.5 "Tiered retention and lifecycle"
417	
418	Reuses the cold object store and search index; adds a lifecycle manager that ages shards down tiers and drops expired partitions.
419	
420	**ARCHITECTURE DECISIONS TABLE**
421	
422	| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off we accept |
423	|---|---|---|---|---|
424	| Lifecycle manager | A scheduler that moves time-shards hot→warm→cold and drops expired ones | At ~260 TB/day, retention is the cost lever; must reclaim space in O(1) | Not row-by-row DELETE (churns the index, never reclaims cleanly) | Old data slower to query (warm/cold) |
425	| Cold object store (S3/Glacier) | Cheap durable storage for compressed raw logs | ~25x cheaper than hot; durable; rehydratable for rare deep queries | Not keeping everything in ES (would be ~$2M/mo just hot) | Cold queries take minutes (rehydrate) |
426	
427	> 🎙️ **Narration:** "Retention is a cost decision dressed as an engineering one. A lifecycle manager moves whole time-based index shards from hot SSD nodes to cheaper warm nodes after a week, archives the compressed raw to Glacier-class object storage, and then drops entire expired partitions in one operation. I never delete logs row by row — at this volume that churns the index and never reclaims space. Cold queries are rare and slow, and that's the deliberate trade-off."
428	
429	> **🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
430	> **Time-partitioning** — splitting data into shards by time window (e.g. one per hour). Here it lets us drop a whole expired window instantly.
431	> **Partition drop** — deleting an entire partition as one cheap metadata operation. Here it reclaims petabytes in O(1) instead of churning through rows.
432	> **Tiering** — moving data across hot/warm/cold storage by age and access frequency. Here it's the difference between ~$420K and several million a month.
433	
434	**DIAGRAM — Lifecycle tiering**

442	    ES -->|"2 move shard"| WARM

451	
452	**NUMBERED STEP NARRATION**
453	1. **Lifecycle manager identifies week-old shards.** *Why:* hot tier is expensive SSD. *State:* shards flagged. *What goes wrong:* misconfigured policy → guardrail blocks dropping anything under min-retention.
454	2. **Shard moves to warm nodes** (cheaper disk, one replica). *State:* still queryable, slower. *What goes wrong:* move fails → shard stays hot, alarmed.
455	3. **Raw archived to cold object store** compressed. *State:* permanent cheap copy. *What goes wrong:* already teed at ingest, so this is idempotent.
456	4. **Expired partition dropped** as one operation. *State:* space reclaimed instantly. *What goes wrong:* dropping live data → partitions are immutable + below min-retention check.
457	
458	> 💬 "The cost story lives here: drop whole partitions, never rows, and let Glacier hold the long tail."
459	
460	> 🎙️ **Script:** "A lifecycle manager ages index shards from hot SSD to warm disk after a week, keeps a compressed copy in cold object storage, and drops entire expired partitions in one cheap operation. The decision is partition-drop over row-delete — at a quarter petabyte a day, anything else churns the index and bleeds money. Cold data is slow to query, and we accept that because nobody interactively searches last March."

504	## 7. Data Model & Storage
505	
506	**PART A — ENTITY TABLE**
507	
508	| Entity | Key fields (PK / shard key annotated) | Chosen store | Shard / partition key | Consistency |
509	|---|---|---|---|---|
510	| LogEvent | event_id (PK), ts, **service (shard)**, level, message, trace_id, fields(json) | Search index + cold object store | by service + time window | Eventual (~5s lag) |
511	| Service | service_id (PK), team_id, quota_eps | Relational (Postgres) | service_id | Strong |
512	| AlertRule | rule_id (PK), target, condition, notify, created_by | Relational (Postgres) | rule_id | Strong |
513	| Aggregate | (service, level, minute) composite PK | Aggregate store (time-series/Redis+TSDB) | service + time | Eventual (~10s) |
514	| RetentionPolicy | policy_id (PK), scope, hot_days, warm_days, cold_days | Relational (Postgres) | policy_id | Strong |
515	
516	**PART B — STORAGE DECISION CARDS**
517	
518	**🗄️ Search index (Elasticsearch / OpenSearch) — used for: LogEvent (hot + warm)**
519	1. **What it is:** a search engine that builds an inverted index — a precomputed map from every word/field-value to the events containing it — so a full-text query is a lookup, not a scan.
520	2. **Why we picked it:** we need ranked full-text + structured-field search over trillions of immutable rows returning in <1s. Only an inverted index does that; the time-sharded layout also makes tiering and partition-drop trivial.
521	3. **Considered instead:**
522	   - **PostgreSQL full-text** — fine at millions of rows, collapses at trillions; no horizontal index sharding for our volume.
523	   - **ClickHouse** — superb for aggregations/scans and cheaper storage, but weaker ad-hoc full-text ranking; a strong alternative I'd revisit if search were mostly analytical, not keyword.
524	   - **Hand-rolled inverted index** — months of work, no relevance ranking, reinventing Lucene.
525	4. **Trade-off:** expensive to store (index overhead) and lags raw by ~5s; mitigated by tiering and sampling.
526	5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "For search I'm using an Elasticsearch-style inverted index because nothing else does sub-second full-text over trillions of logs. I considered ClickHouse — it's cheaper and great at aggregations — and I'd switch if queries were mostly analytical, but for keyword search ES wins. The cost is index storage, which I control with tiering."
527	
528	**🗄️ Kafka — used for: the ingest buffer (transient LogEvent stream)**
529	1. **What it is:** a durable, replicated, append-only log that producers write to and many consumers read from independently at their own pace.
530	2. **Why we picked it:** it's the shock absorber — it soaks the 25M/s burst, decouples agents from the index, lets indexers and the aggregator consume the same stream, and replays on failure.
531	3. **Considered instead:**
532	   - **SQS/Kinesis** — managed and simpler, but lower sustained throughput per cost and weaker multi-consumer replay at this scale.
533	   - **RabbitMQ** — great for routing, not for a multi-GB/s firehose with replay.
534	4. **Trade-off:** brokers are real infrastructure to operate; we accept that for the durability + replay.
535	5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "Kafka is the buffer between a firehose that bursts five-x and an index with a fixed write ceiling. I looked at Kinesis for less ops burden but the throughput-per-dollar and replay story favored Kafka at three gigabytes a second."
536	
537	**🗄️ Cold object store (S3 + Glacier) — used for: raw LogEvent archive**
538	1. **What it is:** cheap, massively durable blob storage for compressed raw logs, ~25x cheaper than hot SSD.
539	2. **Why we picked it:** at 19 PB/year, only object storage is affordable for long-tail compliance retention; it's 11-nines durable and rehydratable for rare deep queries.
540	3. **Considered instead:** keeping everything in ES (~$2M+/mo, absurd); a self-run HDFS cluster (more ops, less durable than S3).
541	4. **Trade-off:** cold queries require rehydration (minutes), which is fine because deep-history search is rare.
542	5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "Anything older than a month goes to Glacier-class storage compressed — twenty-five times cheaper. The trade-off is a slow rehydrate for the rare query into last quarter, and I'm happy to pay that in seconds-of-wait to save millions."
543	
544	**🗄️ Aggregate store (time-series DB + Redis) — used for: Aggregate rollups**
545	1. **What it is:** a small fast store of per-service-per-minute counters, with hot recent windows in Redis and history in a time-series DB.
546	2. **Why we picked it:** alerts and dashboards must read counts in milliseconds; the data is tiny (counters, not logs).
547	3. **Considered instead:** querying ES for counts (self-DDoS at 10K rules); Postgres (fine but slower for time-series rollups at scale).
548	4. **Trade-off:** rollups are lossy summaries by design — you can't drill from a counter to the exact lines without going to the index.
549	5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "Alerts and dashboards read tiny per-minute counters from a time-series store, not raw logs. That's the only way ten thousand rules and two thousand panels stay cheap."
550	
551	**🗄️ Postgres — used for: Service, AlertRule, RetentionPolicy (control plane)**
552	1. **What it is:** a relational database with strong transactions for the small, structured config data.
553	2. **Why we picked it:** rule/service/policy config is low-volume, relational, and needs strong consistency and authz joins.
554	3. **Considered instead:** stuffing config into ES (wrong tool — config isn't searchable logs).
555	4. **Trade-off:** none meaningful at this volume — it's a small control-plane DB.
556	5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "The control plane — services, alert rules, retention policies — is just Postgres. It's small, relational, and needs strong consistency, so I don't overthink it."
557	
558	**PART C — Per-operation consistency**
559	
560	| Operation | Store | Consistency | Why right here |
561	|---|---|---|---|
562	| Ingest a log | Kafka → index | Eventual (~5s) | Decoupling beats instant search; ack on durable buffer |
563	| Create alert rule | Postgres | Strong | Config correctness; must not lose a rule |
564	| Read rollup for alert | Aggregate store | Eventual (~10s) | Counts trail real-time slightly; acceptable for 60s detection |
565	| Search logs | Search index | Eventual | A 5s-old log being absent is fine |
566	| Read-your-own-writes (just-logged event) | index | NOT guaranteed instantly | Within ~5s lag; UI shows "indexing…" hint; tail-from-Kafka option for live tail |
567	
568	**PART D — DATA LIFECYCLE (largest table: LogEvent)**
569	
570	At ~260 TB/day raw, the hot index alone is ~2 PB for 7 days (the $200K/mo line from §3). Retention caps it: **7 days hot, ~30 days warm, 365 days cold in Glacier, then expire.** Reclamation is **partition-drop** on time-sharded indices — dropping a whole day's shard is one O(1) metadata operation, never a row-by-row DELETE (which churns the inverted index and never cleanly reclaims SSD). Cold data lives compressed in object storage and is rehydrated only for rare deep queries. Without tiering, hot storage grows unbounded toward millions of dollars a month.
571	
572	> 🎙️ **Script:** "The core stores are an Elasticsearch-style index for hot and warm searchable logs, Kafka as the durable buffer in front of it, and Glacier-class object storage for the cheap long tail. Alerts and dashboards run off a tiny time-series aggregate store, not the raw index, and the control plane is plain Postgres. The one I'd flag: I considered ClickHouse instead of ES and would switch if the workload were mostly analytical rather than keyword search. And the lifecycle answer for the giant log table is partition-drop on time shards — instant reclamation, never row deletes."

576	## 8. Deep Dives — Bad → Good → Great
577	
578	> 🆘 **If you get stuck:** go back to the binding number — 5M events/s in, 70K reads out, and a fixed index write ceiling — and reason forward from "what do I buffer and what do I drop."
579	
580	### How do we survive the ingestion firehose and its 5x bursts?
581	
582	**#### Bad: Agents write directly to the index**
583	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** every app sends its logs straight into the search engine.
584	- **Approach:** each service POSTs log lines to Elasticsearch as they happen.
585	- **Why people try this:** it's the obvious, fewest-moving-parts design.
586	- ⚠️ **What breaks:** the index has a fixed write ceiling (~15K docs/s/node). At 5M/s you'd need ~330 nodes always, and a 5x burst to 25M/s instantly overruns them — index write queues fill, rejections cascade, and worse, the *apps themselves* slow down because they're blocked writing logs during an incident. The tool fails exactly when needed.
587	- 🔁 **Forces upgrade:** any burst, and the coupling of app health to index health.
588	
589	**#### Good: Buffer in Kafka, index asynchronously**
590	- ↩️ **What the previous tier got wrong:** Bad coupled producers to the index, so a burst or slow index stalled the apps. Good inserts a durable buffer so producers never wait on the index.
591	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** logs go into a big durable waiting line first; the index drains the line at its own pace.
592	- **Approach:** agents → gateway → Kafka (a durable replicated log). Indexer workers consume and bulk-write. A 5x burst inflates the *queue depth*, not the index load; Kafka holds the backlog (sized for ~hours of retention) and indexers catch up after the spike.
593	- **Why reasonable:** decoupling is the standard firehose pattern.
594	- ⚠️ **What breaks:** a *sustained* (not transient) overload — say a bad deploy logging at 10x for an hour — grows Kafka lag unboundedly. Searchable lag blows past 5s to minutes; eventually Kafka retention is exceeded and you lose the *oldest* data silently, which may be the most important.
595	
596	```mermaid
597	flowchart TD
598	    A[Agents] -->|"produce burst"| K[[Kafka buffer]]
599	    K -->|"drain at own pace"| IX[Indexer workers]
600	    IX -->|"bulk index"| ES[(Search index)]
601	    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,color:#e6edf3
602	    classDef queue fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,color:#e6edf3
603	    classDef db fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,color:#e6edf3
604	    class A,IX svc
605	    class K queue
606	    class ES db
607	```
608	- 🔁 **Forces upgrade:** sustained overload that outpaces both indexers and Kafka retention — we need to *choose* what to drop, not lose data randomly.
609	
610	**#### Great: Tiered sampling + back-pressure under sustained overload**
611	- ↩️ **What the previous tier got wrong:** Good would, under sustained overload, silently drop the *oldest* (often most-relevant) data when Kafka retention is exceeded. Great makes dropping a *deliberate, prioritized* decision at the gateway.
612	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** when we're drowning, we throw away the boring logs first and keep the errors, on purpose, and tell the agent we did.
613	- **Approach:** the gateway watches **consumer lag** (how far behind indexers are) and **Kafka queue depth**. Load-shedding order: (1) shed debug/info logs first via head-sampling (keep 1-in-N), (2) keep WARN at reduced rate, (3) **never drop ERROR/FATAL**. The 202 response carries `dropped` counts so agents apply their own back-pressure. Kafka is the bounded buffer — it spills nothing to disk beyond retention; we drop at the gateway *before* Kafka so the buffer protects the high-value path.
614	- **🔢 Decision-forcing math:** headroom-sized fleet ~500 ES nodes x 15K = ~7.5M docs/s sustainable. Average is 5M/s (fits), peak 25M/s does NOT. So at >7.5M/s sustained we sample: keeping 100% of ~10% error logs + sampling the 90% info logs at 1-in-5 brings 25M/s down to ~2.5M error + ~4.5M sampled = ~7M/s — back under ceiling, with zero error loss.
615	- **✅ Failure matrix:**
616	
617	| Scenario | What happens |
618	|---|---|
619	| Transient 5x burst | Kafka absorbs, indexers catch up, no drops |
620	| Sustained 5x for an hour | Gateway samples info/debug, keeps all errors, lag stays bounded |
621	| Indexer fleet loses 30% | Lag rises → sampling kicks in earlier → no cascade |
622	| Kafka broker dies | Replica serves; producer retries idempotent on batch_id |
623	| Gateway overloaded | Returns 429 + Retry-After; agents buffer locally then drop debug |
624	| Agent can't reach gateway | On-host buffer, then drop lowest severity first |
625

627	flowchart TD
628	    A[Agents] -->|"batch"| GW[Ingest gateway]
629	    GW -->|"watch lag"| LAG[Lag signal]
630	    LAG -->|"over ceiling"| GW
631	    GW -->|"keep errors drop info"| K[[Kafka buffer]]
632	    GW -.->|"202 with dropped count"| A
633	    K --> IX[Indexer workers]
634	    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,color:#e6edf3
635	    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,color:#e6edf3
